213.reason: [After detecting a lexical error, the program should terminate immediately to avoid any further processing, but it continues looping through the rest of the input. Although the problem states there is exactly one error, continuing processing is unnecessary and could lead to issues in more complex scenarios. More importantly, if the error occurs near the end of the file, the line number might be miscalculated due to subsequent newline handling, but the primary issue is lack of early termination.]

5.reason: [The error is written immediately upon detection, but the program continues processing the rest of the input. Although the specification states there is exactly one error, continuing may lead to undefined behavior if the input contains additional malformed constructs (even though the problem guarantees only one lexical error). More critically, the line number might become inaccurate if newline characters are processed after the error, but since the error's line number is captured at the time of detection, this is not the issue. However, the fundamental flaw is that the error output is performed directly via IO.write in the middle of lexing, whereas best practice (and possibly the test harness expectation) is to collect errors and write them all at the end. But the problem states only one error, so this should be acceptable. Despite this, given the failure, this line is the only point where error 'a' is output, so any discrepancy in formatting or timing would originate here.]
